options.b: `c` and `d`, because both are `const` and initialized from compile-time values.
options.c: `a`, `b`, `c`, and `d` are all constant expressions since they are all integers.
options.d: None of them; constant expressions require `constexpr`.

explanation_md: `c` is initialized with the literal `10` (a constant expression), and `d` is initialized with `c + 5` where `c` is itself a constant expression — so both qualify. `b` is `const` but initialized from `a`, which is not `const` and therefore not a constant expression, so `b` is not a constant expression either.
`x` is `3`, `y` is `3 + 7 = 10`, `z` is `10 * 2 = 20`. All three are constant expressions: each initializer is computable at compile time. The program prints `20`.

options.a: Yes — `const` variables are always constant expressions.
options.b: No — `score` is not `const`, so its value is not known at compile time, making `passing` not a constant expression.
options.c: Yes — the compiler can see that `score` is `95` and will treat `passing` as a constant expression.
options.d: It depends on whether the compiler optimization level is set to -O2.

explanation_md: `const` means the variable will not be modified after initialization. It does not mean the value is known at compile time. Since `score` is a non-`const` variable, it is not a constant expression, so `passing`'s initializer is not a constant expression even though `passing` itself is `const`.

options.a: The program will not compile because `getMax()` is not declared before `main`.
options.b: `limit` is not a constant expression because `getMax()` is a regular function — its return value is only known at runtime.
options.c: `limit` is a constant expression because `getMax()` always returns `100`.
options.d: The bug is that `const int` should be `const auto` when initialized from a function.

explanation_md: Regular (non-`constexpr`) functions are evaluated at runtime, not at compile time. Even though `getMax()` obviously returns `100`, the compiler does not evaluate its body when determining whether an expression is a constant expression. `limit` is `const` but not a constant expression. The program compiles and runs correctly, but `limit` cannot be used where a compile-time value is required.
`a * b` is `6`, then `6 + 1` is `7`. All three variables are constant expressions: each is initialized from literals or from other constant expressions. The program prints `7`.
